trajectory_summary: The call moved from guarded-but-engaged to booked, which is a real outcome. The customer drove most of the positive momentum herself — she called back, pulled up her Dominion account unprompted, and volunteered availability. Parker held the call together but did not build energy; he managed it. Two structural gaps matter for the closer: the customer's name was never confirmed (Parker called her 'Tracy' at 08:25 and 'Trish' at 10:04 — two different names, neither confirmed in the transcript), and the appointment was set as an in-person visit without the standard phone-appointment protocol, computer-access anchor, or 'is that fair' verbal handshake.
outcome_dispute: The appointment appears booked but is structurally fragile. The button-up is missing the phone-appointment specification, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake — all required per button_up protocol. The in-person framing ('drop by') was never corrected. A booked appointment with an incomplete button-up and an unconfirmed customer name carries elevated no-show risk.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: And then we'll plan to drop by just tomorrow at eleven, kinda run through that design.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Parker booked the appointment but left out three of the five required button-up elements: phone-vs-in-person format clarification, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. On this call the customer was cooperative enough that the booking held anyway — but the same incomplete button-up on a less-committed customer is a no-show. This is the highest-leverage gap because it directly affects whether Parker gets paid on this sit.

key_moments.quote: I saw several advertisements on my Instagram that said that there was, fiscal availability from government or Virginia programs, or Elon Musk was on one, for basically free installation of the solar panels. If that's not true, I'm not interested.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer is not raising a scam accusation — she is raising a misinformation anchor. She has seen Instagram ads using Elon Musk's image to promise free solar. Parker's response at 00:46 ('we do take care of everything up front for you, nothing out of your pocket') is directionally correct on the bill-swap concept but does not acknowledge the misinformation context or validate her skepticism before pivoting. The gap is the missing empathy beat: she was targeted by deceptive advertising and is now on the phone with a solar company. A brief acknowledgment ('those ads are misleading and I understand why you'd be skeptical') before the reframe would have landed the bill-swap concept with more trust.

key_moments.quote: So but you're charging people for the solar power?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the clearest flip opportunity in the call. The customer's frame is: 'you are charging me for something.' The inversion is: 'you are already being charged — by Dominion — and that charge goes up every year.' Parker instead re-explained the payment structure ('the pro how long ago do they get solar... the programs have changed'), which is a defensive answer that stays inside her frame rather than breaking it. The flip was available and unused.

key_moments.quote: Most programs that I know of don't sell back or buy back any electric. It's more of just credits these days. Whereas a couple years ago, maybe five years ago, they used to buy it back a lot. It's just kinda been a changing program since then.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker is explaining net metering policy history — this is closer-tier content. The customer asked about her friends' $8 bills and Dominion buyback. The correct setter move is a brief acknowledgment and a bridge to the specialist: 'Net metering rules vary by utility and have changed — that's exactly what our specialist walks through with the actual numbers for your home.' Instead Parker spent approximately 15 seconds on policy history that (a) he may not have right, (b) is not his job to explain, and (c) did not resolve the customer's underlying concern.

key_moments.quote: And it's a new construction in 2023.
key_moments.stewart_read: New construction in 2023 means the roof is approximately two years old — well within the viable range for solar installation and likely under builder warranty. This is a strong qualification signal that Parker did not anchor on. He responded by looking at satellite imagery and commenting on the neighborhood, which is rapport-adjacent but not value-anchored. The correct move is to name the advantage explicitly: a two-year-old roof is ideal timing for solar because the roof won't need replacement before the system pays back.
